content: Six molecules have speeds 2 units, 5 units, 3 units, 6 units, 3 units and 5 units respectively. The rms speed is
Which one of the figures gives the temperatures dependance of density of water correctly?
explanation: Anomalous density of water is given by (a). It has maximum density at 4$$^\circ$$C. When ice is formed it floats.

content: An experiment takes 10 minutes to raise temperature of water from 0$$^\circ$$C to 100$$^\circ$$C and another 55 minutes to convert it totally into steam by a stabilized heater. The latent heat of vaporization comes out to be
explanation:
Heat given for raising the temperature of W g of water from 0$$^\circ$$C to 100$$^\circ$$C = W $$\times$$ 1 $$\times$$ 100 cal

Time taken = 10 $$\times$$ 60 s.

$$\therefore$$ Heat given per second = $${{W \times 1 \times 100} \over {10 \times 60}}$$ cal

Heat given out to convert W g to steam $$ = W \times L$$

This is the heat supplied in $$50 \times 60$$ s.

$$\therefore$$ Heat given $$ = 100 \times W \times {{55 \times 60} \over {10 \times 60}} = W\,L$$

$$\therefore$$ $$L = {{100 \times 55 \times 60} \over {10 \times 60}} = 100 \times 5.5$$

$$L = 550$$ cal/g

content: At what temperature will the rms speed of air molecules be double that of NTP?
explanation:
$${v_{rms}} = \sqrt {{{3RT} \over M}} $$;

at $$T = {T_0}$$, $${v_{rms}} = \sqrt {{{3R{T_0}} \over M}} $$

But temperature T, $${v_{rms}} = 2 \times \sqrt {{{3R{T_0}} \over M}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow \sqrt {{{3RT} \over M}} = 2\sqrt {{{3R{T_0}} \over M}} \Rightarrow \sqrt T = \sqrt {4{T_0}} $$

or, $$T = 4{T_0}$$

$$T = 4 \times 273\,K = 1092\,K$$

$$\therefore$$ $$T = 819^\circ C$$

content: When the room temperature becomes equal to the dew point, the relative humidity of the room is
explanation:
Dew point is the temperature at which the air can no longer hold any more water vapor. When the room temperature becomes equal to the dew point, the air is saturated with water vapor and the relative humidity is 100%.

If the room temperature is below the dew point, the air cannot hold all of the water vapor that is present and some of the water vapor will condense out of the air. This is why you often see dew on surfaces in the morning when the temperature is cool and the air is humid.

If the room temperature is above the dew point, the air can hold more water vapor than is currently present. This is why you often see fog in the morning when the temperature is cool and the air is humid. The fog is caused by the water vapor in the air condensing out of the air onto cold surfaces.

content: For an ideal gas, a cyclic process ABCA as shown in P-T diagram, when presented in P-V plot, would be
explanation:
Given, $p-T$ diagram for cyclic process $A B C A$ Use ideal gas law.

$$ V \propto \frac{T}{p} $$

Process analysis $A B: p$ constant, $T \downarrow \Rightarrow V \downarrow \rightarrow$ isobaric compression $B C: T$ constant, $p \downarrow \Rightarrow V \uparrow \rightarrow$ isothermal expansion $C A: p \uparrow, T \uparrow \Rightarrow V$ constant.

From the options, only option (c) shows $A B$ as horizontal line (constant $p$ ), $B C$ as smooth curve (isothermal), $C A$ as vertical line (constant $V$ )

content: Temperature of a body $\theta$ is slightly more than the temperature of the surrounding $\theta_0$. Its rate of cooling $(R)$ versus temperature of the body $(\theta)$ is plotted. Its shape would be
explanation:
Newton's law of cooling,

$$ R=k\left(\theta-\theta_0\right) $$

where, $k$ is a positive constant.

Clearly, as $\theta$ increases, $R$ also increases linearly.

content: The speed distribution for a sample of $$\mathrm{N}$$ gas particles is shown below. $$\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{v})=0$$ for $$\mathrm{v}>2 \mathrm{v}_0$$. How many particles have speeds between $$1.2 \mathrm{v}_0$$ and $$1.8 \mathrm{v}_0$$ ?
explanation:
$\begin{aligned} \text { Favourable space } & =\int\limits_{1.2 v_0}^{1.8 v_0} P d v \\\\ & =a \times 0.6 v_0\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} \text { Sample space } & =\int\limits_0^{\infty} P d v \\ & =\text { Area } \\\\ & =\frac{1}{2} v_0 a+\left(2 v_0-v_0\right) a \\\\ & =1.5 a v_0 \\\\ \frac{n}{N} & =\frac{0.6}{1.6}=\frac{2}{5} \\\\ n & =\frac{2}{5} N=0.4 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

content: The internal energy of a thermodynamic system is given by $$U=a s^{4 / 3} V^\alpha$$ where $$\mathrm{s}$$ is entropy, $$\mathrm{V}$$ is volume and '$$\mathrm{a}$$' and '$$\alpha$$' are constants. The value of $$\alpha$$ is
explanation:
We know that for a system, the internal energy (U) is a function of entropy (S) and volume (V). We can write this as:

$$dU = TdS - PdV$$

Where:

  • T is the temperature
  • P is the pressure

Now, let's differentiate the given expression for internal energy:

$$dU = \frac{4}{3}as^{1/3}V^\alpha dS + a\alpha s^{4/3}V^{\alpha-1}dV$$

Comparing the coefficients of dS and dV in both expressions, we get:

$$T = \frac{4}{3}as^{1/3}V^\alpha$$

$$P = -a\alpha s^{4/3}V^{\alpha-1}$$

Now, let's find the relation between T, P, V, and S using the following thermodynamic relations:

$$T = \left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial S} \right)_V$$

$$P = -\left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial V} \right)_S$$

From the expression for T, we have:

$$T = \frac{4}{3}as^{1/3}V^\alpha$$

From the expression for P, we have:

$$P = -a\alpha s^{4/3}V^{\alpha-1}$$

Now, let's substitute the expressions for T and P into the relation $$T = \left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial S} \right)_V$$ and $$P = -\left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial V} \right)_S$$:

For $$T = \left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial S} \right)_V$$:

$$ \frac{4}{3}as^{1/3}V^\alpha = \frac{4}{3}as^{1/3}V^\alpha $$

For $$P = -\left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial V} \right)_S$$:

$$ -a\alpha s^{4/3}V^{\alpha-1} = -a\alpha s^{4/3}V^{\alpha-1} $$

We can see that both equations are consistent. Therefore, the value of $$\alpha$$ can be any number. However, in the context of thermodynamics, the internal energy is often expressed as a function of entropy and volume, where the exponent of volume is often -1/3. This leads to the following expression for internal energy:

$$U = as^{4/3}V^{-1/3}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is D. $$-\frac{1}{3}$$

content: Six molecules of an ideal gas have velocities 1, 3, 5, 5, 6 and 5 m/s respectively. At any given temperature, if $$\mathrm{\overline V}$$ and $$\mathrm{V_{rms}}$$ represent average and rms speed of the molecules, then
explanation:
The velocities of the molecules of ideal gas are $$1,3,5,5,6$$ and $$5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$.

$$\therefore$$ Average speed,

$$\bar{v}=\frac{\sum v}{n}=\frac{1+3+5+5+6+5}{6} \Rightarrow \frac{25}{6}=4.1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$

Root mean square speed, $$v_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{\Sigma v^2}{n}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore v_{\mathrm{rms}} & =\sqrt{\frac{1^2+3^2+5^2+5^2+6^2+5^2}{6}} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{121}{6}}=\frac{11 \sqrt{6}}{6}=4.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ \therefore v_{\mathrm{rms}} & >\bar{v} \end{aligned}$$

content:
A given quantity of gas is taken from A to C in two ways; a) directly from A $$\to$$ C along a straight line and b) in two steps, from A $$\to$$ B and then from B $$\to$$ C. Work done and heat absorbed along the direct path A $$\to$$ C is 200 J and 280 J respectively.

If the work done along A $$\to$$ B $$\to$$ C is 80 J, then heat absorbed along this path is,
explanation:
Given,

$$\begin{aligned} & W_{A C}=200 \mathrm{~J} \\ & Q_{A C}=280 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

By first law of thermodynamics

$$\begin{aligned} & Q_{A C}=\Delta U_{A C}+W_{A C} \\ & \therefore \quad \Delta U_{A C}=Q_{A C}-W_{A C} \\ &=280-200=80 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \therefore \quad \Delta U_{A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C}=80 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \text { From process } A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C, \\ & W_{A B C}=80 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ By first law of thermodynamics

$$\begin{aligned} Q_{A B C} & =\Delta U_{A B C}+W_{A B C} \\ & =80+80=160 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

content: Two substances A and B of same mass are heated at constant rate. The variation of temperature $$\theta$$ of the substances with time t is shown in the figure. Choose the correct statement.

options.content: Specific heat of A is greater than that of B.
Specific heat of B is greater than that of A.
None of the above is true.

explanation:
Given, $$\frac{d H}{d t}=$$ constant $$(K)$$ ..... (i)

Where, $$H=m s \theta$$

$$\therefore$$ From Eq. (i),

$$\begin{aligned} & m s \frac{d \theta}{d t}=K \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d \theta}{d t} \propto \frac{1}{s} \end{aligned}$$

i.e., slope of $$\theta$$ vs $$t$$ graph $$\propto \frac{1}{s}$$

As (Slope)$$_B$$ > (Slope)$$_A$$

$$\therefore(\text { Specific heat })_A>(\text { Specific heat })_B$$

content: Certain amount of an ideal gas is taken from its initial state 1 to final state 4 through the paths 1 $$\to$$ 2 $$\to$$ 3 $$\to$$ 4 as shown in figure. AB, CD, EF are all isotherms. If vp is the most probable speed of the molecules, then

options.content: v<sub>p</sub> at 3 = v<sub>p</sub> at 4 > v<sub>p</sub> at 2 > v<sub>p</sub> at 1
v<sub>p</sub> at 3 > v<sub>p</sub> at 1 > v<sub>p</sub> at 2 > v<sub>p</sub> at 4
v<sub>p</sub> at 3 > v<sub>p</sub> at 2 > v<sub>p</sub> at 4 > v<sub>p</sub> at 1
v<sub>p</sub> at 2 = v<sub>p</sub> at 3 > v<sub>p</sub> at 1 > v<sub>p</sub> at 4

explanation:
For the given isotherm, the temperature of the curve EF is greater than the curve CD and AB.

As we know that, most probable speed of the molecule is given as

$${v_p} = \sqrt {{{2RT} \over M}} $$

Thus, the $${v_p}$$ at 3 and 4 will be same.

Hence, $${v_p}$$ at 3 = $${v_p}$$ at 4 > $${v_p}$$ at 2 > $${v_p}$$ at 1.

content: Consider a thermodynamic process where integral energy $$U = A{P^2}V$$ (A = constant). If the process is performed adiabatically, then
explanation:
$$U = A{p^2}V$$, $$A = $$ constant

For adiabatic process, $$dQ = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ From first law of thermodynamics,

$$dQ = dU + dW$$

$$\therefore$$ $$dU + dW = 0$$ ($$\because$$ $$dQ = 0$$)

$$ \Rightarrow d(A{p^2}V) + pdV = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow A(2p\,.\,Vdp + {p^2}dV) + pdV = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (A{p^2} + p)dV + 2ApVdp = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (A{p^2} + p)dV = - 2ApVdp$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{dV} \over { - 2AV}} = {{pdp} \over {p(Ap + 1)}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{dp} \over {(Ap + 1)}} + {1 \over {2A}}\,.\,{{dv} \over v} = 0$$

Integrating on both sides,

$$ \Rightarrow \int {{{dp} \over {(Ap + 1)}} + {1 \over {2A}}\int {{{dV} \over V} = \int 0 } } $$

$$ \Rightarrow {{\ln (Ap + 1)} \over A} + {1 \over {2A}}\ln V = \ln C$$

$$ \Rightarrow \ln (Ap + 1) + \ln {V^{1/2}} = \ln C$$

$$ \Rightarrow \ln [{V^{1/2}}(Ap + 1)] = \ln C$$

$$ \Rightarrow {V^{1/2}}(Ap + 1) = C$$

$$ \Rightarrow V{(Ap + 1)^2} = C$$ (constant)

content: One mole of a diatomic ideal gas undergoes a process shown in P-V diagram. The total heat given to the gas (ln 2 = 0.7) is
explanation:
The given process BC is isothermal and AB is isochoric.

From A to B, we have

$${p_0} \to 2{p_0}$$

as $$p \propto T$$

Hence, $${T_B} = 2{T_A} = 2{T_0}$$

$${Q_{AB}} = \Delta {U_{AB}} = {W_{AB}} = {C_V}\Delta T + 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {Q_{AB}} = {5 \over 2}R(2{T_0} - {T_0}) = {5 \over 2}{p_0}{V_0}$$

($$\because$$ $${C_V} = {5 \over 2}R$$ for diatomic gas and $$pV = RT$$)

From B to C, we have,

$${Q_{BC}} = \Delta {U_{BC}} + {W_{BC}} = 0 + R(2{T_0})\ln {{2{V_0}} \over {{V_0}}}$$

$$ = 2R{T_0}\ln 2$$

$$ = 2\ln 2{p_0}{V_0}$$

Hence, $${Q_{total}} = {Q_{AB}} + {Q_{BC}}$$

$$ = (2.5 + 2\ln 2){p_0}{V_0}$$

$$ = 3.9\,{p_0}{V_0}$$

content: One mole of an ideal monoatomic gas expands along the polytrope PV3 = constant from V1 to V2 at a constant pressure P1. The temperature during the process is such that molar specific heat $${C_V} = {{3R} \over 2}$$. The total heat absorbed during the process can be expressed as
explanation:
Given $$p{V^3} = C,\,{C_V} = {{3R} \over 2},\,n = 1$$ mol

For polytropic process,

$$p{V^x} = C$$

$$\therefore$$ $$x = 3$$

$$C = {C_V} + {R \over {1 - x}} = {{3R} \over 2} + {R \over {1 - 3}} = R$$

Heat supplied $$(Q) = nC\Delta T$$

$$ = 1 \times R \times ({T_2} - {T_1}) = R({T_2} - {T_1})$$

Now, As $$pV = nRT$$ ...... (i)

$$\therefore$$ $$pV = RT$$ ($$\because$$ $$n = 1$$)

$$\therefore$$ $$R = {{pV} \over T} = {{{p_1}{T_1}} \over {{T_1}}}$$

Also, $$p{V^3} = C$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left( {{{RT} \over V}} \right){V^3} = C$$ [from Eq. (i)]

$$ \Rightarrow T{V^2} = C \Rightarrow {T_1}V_1^2 = {T_2}V_2^2$$

$$\therefore$$ $${T_2} = {{{T_1}V_1^2} \over {V_2^2}}$$

Heat supplied $$(Q) = {{{p_1}{V_1}} \over {{T_1}}}\left[ {{{{T_1}V_1^2} \over {V_2^2}} - {T_1}} \right] = {p_1}{V_1}\left[ {{{V_1^2} \over {V_2^2}} - 1} \right]$$

content: In the given figure, 1 represents isobaric, 2 represents isothermal and 3 represents adiabatic processes of an ideal gas. If $$\Delta$$U1, $$\Delta$$U2 and $$\Delta$$U3 be the changes in internal energy in these processes respectively, then
explanation:
Given figure is


Process 1 is isobaric expansion (p = constant). Hence, temperature of gas will increase, therefore $$\Delta$$U1 will be positive.

Process 2 is an isothermal process, $$\Delta$$U2 = 0

Process 3 is an adiabatic expansion, hence temperature of gas will fall.

$$\therefore$$ $$\Delta$$U3 = negative

Therefore, $$\Delta$$1 > $$\Delta$$U2 > $$\Delta$$U3

content: If pressure of real gas O<sub>2</sub>, in a container is given by $$p = {{RT} \over {2V - b}} - {a \over {4{b^2}}}$$, then the mass of the gas in the container is
explanation:
The van der Waals' equation for real gas is as follows

$$p + {{a{n^2}} \over {{V^2}}}(V - nb) = nRT$$

where, p is the pressure, a and b are constants, V is the volume, T is the temperature and n is the number of moles.

van der Waals' equation can be rearranged as

$$p = {{nRT} \over {V - nb}} - {{a{n^2}} \over {{V^2}}}$$

Dividing first part with n and the 2nd part with n2, we get

$$p = {{{{nRT} \over n}} \over {{V \over n} - {{nb} \over n}}} - {{{{a{n^2}} \over {{n^2}}}} \over {{{{V^2}} \over {{n^2}}}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow p = {{RT} \over {{V \over n} - b}} - {a \over {{{{V^2}} \over {{n^2}}}}}$$ ..... (i)

The equation given in question is as follows

$$p = {{RT} \over {2V - b}} - {a \over {4{b^2}}}$$ ..... (ii)

By comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$${V \over n} = 2v$$

$$ \Rightarrow n = {V \over {2V}} = {1 \over 2}$$ (i.e. $$n = {1 \over 2}$$ mole)

By using mole formula to determine the amount of O2 as follows

$$Mole = {{Mass} \over {Molar\,mass}}$$ ...... (iii)

The molar mass of O2 is 32 g/mol. Substituting in Eq. (iii), we get

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over 2} = {{Mass} \over {32g/mol}}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ Mass of O2 = 16 g

content: 300 g of water at 25$$^\circ$$C is added to 100 g of ice at 0$$^\circ$$C. The final temperature of the mixture is
explanation:
We know that, latent heat of fusion at ice is 79.7 cal per gram.

Let final temperature be T.

Then,

m1s$$\Delta$$T = m2L

$$\Rightarrow$$ 300 $$\times$$ 1 $$\times$$ (25 $$-$$ T) = 100 $$\times$$ 75

$$\Rightarrow$$ 25 $$-$$ T = 25

$$\Rightarrow$$ T = 0$$^\circ$$C

After that total energy left, Q = 4.7 $$\times$$ 100 = 470 cal

Total mass of water = 400 g

Amount of water again converted into ice,

$$m = {Q \over {{L_{ice}}}} = {{470} \over {79.7}}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ m = 5.9 g

Thus, whole mass is converted into water at 0$$^\circ$$C and about 5.9 g water is again converted into ice whose temperature is also 0$$^\circ$$C.

After achieving the temperature of 0$$^\circ$$C, latent heat of fusion is required firstly for conversion of water into ice, then further lowering of temperature as possible. So, the final temperature will be 0$$^\circ$$C.

content:
An ideal gas undergoes the cyclic process abca as shown in the given p - V diagram


It rejects 50J of heat during ab and absorbs 80J of heat during ca. During bc, there is no transfer of heat and 40J of work is done by the gas. What should be the area of the closed curved abca?
explanation:
Given, during ab, heat rejected, Qab = $$ - $$ 50J

During ca, heat absorbed, Qca = 80J

During bc, heat transferred, Qbc = 0J

Work done by the gas, W = 40J

$$ \therefore $$Area of closed curve abca = Qab + Qbc + Qca

Area = $$ - $$ 50 + 0 + 80

Area = 30J

content:
A container AB in the shape of a rectangular parallelopiped of length 5 m is divided internally by a movable partition P as shown in the figure.


The left compartment is filled with a given mass of an ideal gas of molar mass 32 while the right compartment is filled with an equal mass of another ideal gas of molar mass 18 at same temperature. What will be the distance of P from the left wall A when equilibrium is established?
explanation:
Given, length of rectangular container, l = 5m

The number of moles of two gases are

$$\mu $$1 = $${M \over {32}}$$ and $$\mu $$2 = $${M \over {18}}$$

The given situation is shown in the figure given below.


where, x is the distance of movable partition P from the left wall A.

At equilibrium, p1 = p2 = p

and T1 = T2 = T

By ideal gas equation,

$${{{p_1}{V_1}} \over {{\mu _1}}} = {{{p_2}{V_2}} \over {{\mu _2}}} = RT$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{V_1}} \over {{\mu _1}}} = {{{V_2}} \over {{\mu _2}}}$$ ...................[$$ \because $$ p1 = p2]

$${{a.x} \over {{\mu _1}}} = {{a.(5 - x)} \over {{\mu _2}}}$$ [$$ \because $$ a = area of each wall A and B]

$${x \over {{\mu _1}}} = {{5 - x} \over {{\mu _2}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x \over {M/32}} = {{5 - x} \over {M/18}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 32x = 18(5 - x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 32x = 90 $$ - $$ 18x

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 50x = 90

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$x = {{90} \over {50}} = 1.8m$$

content:
When 100 g of boiling water at 100$$^\circ $$
C is added into a calorimeter containing 300 g of cold water at 10$$^\circ $$
C, temperature of the mixture becomes 20$$^\circ $$
C. Then, a metallic block of mass 1 kg at 10$$^\circ $$
C is dipped into the mixture in the calorimeter. After reaching thermal equilibrium, the final temperature becomes 19$$^\circ $$
C. What is the specific heat of the metal in CGS unit?
explanation:
Given, mass of boiling water = 100 g

Mass of cold water = 300 g

Fall in temperature = 100 $$ - $$ 20 = 80$$^\circ $$ C

Let heat capacity of calorimeter = ms

Specific heat of water = 1 cal g-1 $$^\circ $$ C-1

$$ \because $$ Heat lost by boiling water = $$\Delta $$ T = 100 $$ \times $$ 1 $$ \times $$ 80

and heat gained by cold water

= 300 $$ \times $$ 10 $$ \times $$ 10 + ms $$ \times $$ 10

Now, according to the principle of calorimetry,

heat lost = heat gained

$$100 \times 1 \times 80 = 300 \times 1 \times 10 + ms \times 10$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ ms = 500 cal/$$^\circ$$ C

Let specific heat of metal is sb

Mass of block = 1 kg = 1000 g

Rise in temperature of metallic block,

$$\Delta $$ t = 19 $$ - $$ 10 = 9$$^\circ $$ C

Then, again from principle of calorimetry,

mmixture $$ \times $$ s$$\Delta $$ T + ms$$\Delta $$ T = mblock $$ \times $$ sb $$ \times $$ $$\Delta $$ t

$$(100 + 300) \times 1 \times (20 - 19) + 500 \times (20 - 19)$$

= 1000 $$ \times $$ sb $$ \times $$ 9

$$ \Rightarrow $$ sb = 0.1 cal/g$$^\circ $$ C

content: A metallic block of mass 20 kg is dragged with a uniform velocity of 0.5 ms<sup>-1</sup> on a horizontal table for 2.1 s. The coefficient of static friction between the block and the table is 0.10. What will be the maximum possible rise in temperature of the metal block, if the specific heat of the block is 0.1 CGS unit? Assume g = 10 ms<sup>-2</sup> and uniform rise in temperature throughout the whole block. [Ignore absorption of heat by the table]
explanation:
Due to dragging movement of the block, work (wf) will be done against force of friction (f).

Now, force of friction, f = $$\mu $$mg

where, $$\mu $$ = coefficient of static friction = 0.1,

mass, m = 20 kg and g = 10 ms-2.

$$ \Rightarrow $$ f = 0.1 $$ \times $$ 20 $$ \times $$ 10 = 20 N

This work done (Wf) will produce heat energy, H = mc$$\Delta $$ T

Hence, Wf = H

$$ \Rightarrow $$ Force $$ \times $$ Displacement = mc$$\Delta $$ T

$$ \Rightarrow $$ f $$ \times $$ vt = mc$$\Delta $$ T ............[$$ \because $$ s = v . t]

Here, v = 0.5 ms-1, t = 2.1 s

and c = 0.1 CGS unit = 0.1 $$ \times $$ 4.2 $$ \times $$ 103 SI unit

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 20 $$ \times $$ 0.5 $$ \times $$ 2.1 = 20 $$ \times $$ 0.1 $$ \times $$ 4.2 $$ \times $$ 103 $$ \times $$ $$\Delta $$ T

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\Delta $$ T = $${{20 \times 0.5 \times 2.1} \over {20 \times 0.1 \times 4.2 \times {{10}^3}}} = 0.0025^\circ C$$

content: Two black bodies A and B have equal surface areas are maintained at temperatures 27$$^\circ$$C and 177$$^\circ$$C respectively. What will be the ratio of the thermal energy radiated per second by A to that by B?
explanation:
According to the question,

Area of both bodies A and B = A

Temperature of body A = 27$$^\circ$$C = 27 + 273 K

Temperature of body B = 177$$^\circ$$C = 177 + 273 K

Now, by Stefan-Boltzmann law, thermal energy radiated per second by a body

Q = $$\sigma$$AT4,

where, A = Area

T = Temperature

and $$\sigma$$ = Stefan-Boltzmann's constant

So, the ratio of thermal energy radiated per second by A that by B is

$${{{Q_1}} \over {{Q_2}}} = {{\sigma A} \over {\sigma A}}{\left( {{{{T_1}} \over {{T_2}}}} \right)^4}$$

Now, $${{{Q_1}} \over {{Q_2}}} = {\left( {{{{T_1}} \over {{T_2}}}} \right)^4}$$

$${{{Q_1}} \over {{Q_2}}} = {\left( {{{273 + 27} \over {273 + 177}}} \right)^4}$$

$${{{Q_1}} \over {{Q_2}}} = {\left( {{{300} \over {450}}} \right)^4}$$

$${{{Q_1}} \over {{Q_2}}} = {\left( {{2 \over 3}} \right)^4} = \left( {{{16} \over {81}}} \right)$$

Ratio of thermal energy radiated per second

$${Q_1}:{Q_2} = 16:81$$

content: What will be the molar specific heat at constant volume of an ideal gas consisting of rigid diatomic molecules?
explanation:
For a gas at temperature T, the internal energy,

$$U = {f \over 2}\mu RT$$

where, f = degree of freedom

$$ \Rightarrow $$ Change in energy, $$\Delta U = {f \over 2}\mu R\Delta T$$ .... (i)

Also, as we know for any gas heat supplied at constant volume

$$(\Delta {Q_v}) = \mu {C_v}\Delta T = \Delta U$$ .... (ii)

where, Cv = molar specific heat at constant volume

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$${C_v} = {1 \over 2}fR$$

For diatomic gas, degree of freedom, f = 5

$${C_v} = {5 \over 2}R$$

content: Consider the given diagram. An ideal gas is contained in a chamber (left) of volume V and is at an absolute temperature T. It is allowed to rush freely into the right chamber of volume V which is initially vacuum. The whole system is thermally isolated. What will be the final temperature of the equilibrium has been attained?
explanation:
Since, the whole system is isolated, this means, there is no transfer of heat between the system and the surrounding. Also, the right side container is initially vacuum, so the gas could easily crush there without any resistive force. As the right side container has no temperature. Thus, the temperature of the ideal gas would remain same even if it enters right side chamber.

Therefore, final temperature attained at the equilibrium will be T.

content: A horizontal fire hose with a nozzle of cross-sectional area $${5 \over {\sqrt {21} }} \times {10^{ - 3}}{m^2}$$ delivers a cubic metre of water in 10s. What will be the maximum possible increase in the temperature of water while it hits a rigid wall (neglecting the effect of gravity)?
explanation:
Given, cross sectional area of nozzle $$A = {5 \over {\sqrt {21} }} \times {10^{ - 3}}{m^2}$$

and rate of heat transfer $$Q = {1 \over {10}} = {10^{ - 1}}{m^3}/s$$

$$ \because $$ Heat transfer Q = AV

$$ \therefore $$ $$V = {Q \over A} = {{{{10}^{ - 1}}{m^3}/s} \over {{5 \over {\sqrt {21} }} \times {{10}^{ - 3}}{m^2}}} = 2\sqrt {21} \times 10m/s$$

When in hits a rigid wall then maximum possible increase in temperature of water can be expressed as

$${1 \over 2}m{v^2} = ms\Delta T$$ ...(i)

where, m = mass, s = specific heat of water = 1 cal / g = 4.2 $$ \times $$ 103 J / kg and $$\Delta T$$ = increase in temperature

From Eq. (i), $$\Delta T = {1 \over 2}{{{v^2}} \over s}$$

$$ = {{{{(2\sqrt {21} \times {{10}^1})}^2}} \over {2 \times 4.2 \times {{10}^3}}}$$

$$ = {{84 \times {{10}^2}} \over {8.4 \times {{10}^3}}}$$

= $$1^\circ $$C

content: Two identical blocks of ice move in opposite directions with equal speed and collide with each other. What will be the minimum speed required to make both the blocks melt completely, if the initial temperatures of the blocks were $$-$$8$$^\circ$$C each? (Specific heat of ice is 2100 Jkg<sup>$$-$$1</sup>K<sup>$$-$$1</sup> and latent heat of fusion of ice is 3.36 $$ \times $$ 10<sup>5</sup> Jkg<sup>$$-$$1</sup>)
explanation:
Maximum loss in K.E. = Total K.E. energy before collision of two ice blocks

$$K.E{._{\max \,loss}} = {1 \over 2}m{v^2} + {1 \over 2}m{v^2} = m{v^2}$$

This maximum loss in kinetic energy will be equal to loss in heat to melt two ice block.

$$ \Rightarrow m{v^2} = (ms\Delta \theta + mL)2$$ .... (i)

According to question,

$$L = 3.36 \times {10^5}J\,k{g^{ - 1}}$$

$$S = 2100J\,k{g^{ - 1}}{K^{ - 1}}$$

$$\Delta \theta = 8^\circ C$$

From Eq. (i) $$v = \sqrt {2(s\Delta \theta + L)} $$

$$ = \sqrt {2(2100 \times 8 + 3.36 \times {{10}^5})} $$

$$v = 840\,m{s^{ - 1}}$$

content: The water equivalent of a calorimeter is 10 g and it contains 50 g of water at 15$$^\circ$$C. Some amount of ice, initially at $$-$$ 10$$^\circ$$C is dropped in it and half of the ice melts till equilibrium is reached. What was the initial amount of ice that was dropped (when specific heat of ice = 0.5 cal gm<sup>$$-$$1</sup> $$^\circ$$C<sup>$$-$$1</sup>, specific heat of water = 10 cal gm<sup>$$-$$1</sup> $$^\circ$$C<sup>$$-$$1</sup> and latent heat of melting of ice = 80 cal gm<sup>$$-$$1</sup>) ?
explanation:
Let the mass of ice = m

Applying calorimetry principle,

heat given = heat taken

$$({m_1} + {m_2}){s_1}({t_1} - t) = {{mL} \over 2} + m{s_2}(t - {t_2})$$

Putting values, we get

$$(10 + 50) \times 1 \times (15 - 0) = {m \over 2} \times 80 + m \times 0.5[0 - ( - 10)]$$

$$ \Rightarrow 60 \times 15 = 40m + {m \over 2} \times 10 = 45$$m

$$ \therefore $$ $$m = {{60 \times 15} \over {45}} = 20$$g

content: One mole of a monoatomic ideal gas undergoes a quasistatic process, which is depicted by a straight line joining points $$({V_0},{T_0})$$ and $$({2V_0},{3T_0})$$ in a V-T diagram. What is the value of the heat capacity of the gas at the point $$({V_0},{T_0})$$?
explanation:
Heat capacity of an ideal gas in a thermodynamic process,

$$ \because $$ Ideal gas is monoatomic,

$${C_V} = {{fR} \over 2} = {{3R} \over 2}$$

$${C_{process}} = {C_V} + {p \over n}\,.\,{{dV} \over {dT}}$$

$$ = {3 \over 2}R + {p \over n}\,.\,{{{V_0}} \over {2T}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${C_{process}} = {3 \over 2}R + {{nRT} \over {n2T}}$$ [$$ \because $$ pV = nRT]

$$ = {3 \over 2}R + {R \over 2} = 2R$$

content: For an ideal gas with initial pressure and volume p<sub>i</sub> and V<sub>i</sub> respectively, a reversible isothermal expansion happens, when its volume becomes V<sub>0</sub>. Then, it is compressed to its original volume V<sub>i</sub> by a reversible adiabatic process. If the final pressure is p<sub>f</sub>, then which of the following statement(s) is/are true?
explanation:
In p-V diagram, the slope of an adiabatic curve at any point in steeper than that of isothermal curve at that point.


Thus pf > pi

content: Temperature of an ideal gas, initially at 27$$^\circ$$C, is raised by 6$$^\circ$$C. The rms velocity of the gas molecules will
explanation:
Initial temperature of ideal gas,

T1 = 273 + 27 = 300 K

when temperature of gas is raised by 6$$^\circ$$C, the final temperature of gas

T2 = 273 + 6 + 27

= 306 K

Let initial velocities are $${{v_{rm{s_1}}}}$$ and $${{v_{rm{s_2}}}}$$

$${v_{rms}} \propto \sqrt T $$

$${{{v_{rm{s_1}}}} \over {{v_{rm{s_2}}}}} = \sqrt {{{{T_1}} \over {{T_2}}}} $$

$${v_{rm{s_2}}} = \sqrt {{{{T_2}} \over {{T_1}}}} \times {v_{rm{s_1}}}$$

$$ = \sqrt {{{306} \over {300}}} \times {v_{rm{s_1}}}$$

$$ = 1.00 \times {v_{rm{s_1}}}$$

So, it will increase by 1%

content: 2 moles of an ideal monoatomic gas is carried from a state (p<sub>0</sub>, V<sub>0</sub>) to state (2p<sub>0</sub>, 2V<sub>0</sub>) along a straight line path in a p-V diagram. The amount of heat absorbed by the gas in the process is given by
explanation:
The internal energy

$$\Delta U = n{C_v}\Delta T$$

Cv = Specific heat of gas at constant volume

$$ \Rightarrow \Delta U = n.{{3R} \over 2}\left( {{{4{p_0}{V_0}} \over {nR}} - {{{p_0}{V_0}} \over {nR}}} \right)$$

$$ = n.{{3R} \over 2}\left( {{{4{p_0}{V_0} - {p_0}{V_0}} \over {nR}}} \right)$$

$$ = n.{{3R} \over 2}.{{3{p_0}{V_0}} \over {nR}}$$

$$ = {9 \over 2}{p_0}{V_0}$$ ..... (i)

Work done by the gas

$$W = (2{p_0} + {p_0}){{{V_0}} \over 2} = {{3{p_0}{V_0}} \over 2}$$ .... (ii)

From first law of thermodynamics,

$$\Delta Q = dW + dU$$


$$ = {{3{p_0}{V_0}} \over 2} + {9 \over 2}{p_0}{V_0}$$ [from Eqs. (i) and (ii)]

$$ = {{3{p_0}{V_0}} \over 2} + {9 \over 2}{p_0}{V_0}$$

$$ = {{12{p_0}{V_0}} \over 2} = 6{p_0}{V_0}$$

content: The perfect gas equation for 4g of hydrogen gas is
explanation:
Mass of gas = 4g (given)

From perfect gas equation

pV = nRT

where n = number of moles

here, $$\left( {n = {4 \over 2} = 2} \right)$$

$$ \therefore $$ pV = 2RT

It will be the perfect gas equation for 4g of hydrogen.

content: If the temperature of the Sun gets doubled, the rate of energy received on the Earth will increase by a factor of
explanation:
Suppose temperature of Sun = T

When it is doubled, T' = 2T

From law of radiation (Stefan's law)

E $$ \propto $$ T4 = $$\sigma$$T4

$$\sigma$$ = Stefan's constant.

E1 $$ \propto $$ T4

E2 $$ \propto $$ (2T)4

$${{{E_1}} \over {{E_2}}} = {{{T^4}} \over {{2^4}.{T^4}}}$$

E2 = 16 E1

content: If the rms velocity of hydrogen gas at a certain temperature is c, then the rms velocity of oxygen gas at the same temperature is
explanation:
$${v_{rms}} = \sqrt {{{3RT} \over M}} $$

R = gas constant

T = absolute temperature

M = mass of gas

$${v_{rms}}$$ of $${H_2} = \sqrt {{{3RT} \over {{M_{{H_2}}}}}} $$

$${v_{rms}}$$ of $${O_2} = \sqrt {{{3RT} \over {{M_{{O_2}}}}}} $$

$${{{v_{rms}}.{H_2}} \over {{v_{rms}}.{O_2}}} = \sqrt {{{{M_{{O_2}}}} \over {{M_{{H_1}}}}}} $$

Given, $${v_{rms}}{H_2} = C$$

$$ \Rightarrow {C \over {{v_{rms}}{O_2}}} = \sqrt {{{{M_{{O_2}}}} \over {{M_{{H_1}}}}}} = \sqrt {{{32} \over 2}} = \sqrt {16} = 4$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${v_{rms}} = {c \over 4}$$

content: The temperature of the water of a pond is 0$$^\circ$$C while that of the surrounding atmosphere is $$-$$20$$^\circ$$C. If the density of ice is $$\rho$$, coefficient of thermal conductivity is k and latent heat of melting is L, then the thickness Z of ice layer formed increases as a function of time t is
explanation:
Temperature of water = 0$$^\circ$$C

Temperature of surrounding atmosphere = $$-$$ 20$$^\circ$$C

Density of ice = $$\rho$$



Coefficient of thermal conductivity = k

latent heat of melting = L

The rate of heat flow $$H = {{dQ} \over {dt}} = {{kA({T_1} - {T_2})} \over x}$$

$$ = {{kA[(0 - ( - 20)]} \over x}$$

$$ = {{kA \times 20} \over x}$$

$${{dQ} \over {dt}} = {{dm} \over {dt}}.L$$ ($$ \because $$ $$mL = Q$$)

$$dm = \rho A.dx$$

$${{dQ} \over {dt}} = \rho A.{{dx} \over {dt}}L = {{kA.20} \over x}$$

$$\int_0^z {x.\,dx} = {{20k} \over {\rho L}}\int_0^t {dt} \Rightarrow {Z^2} = {{40k} \over {\rho L}}t$$

So, thickness Z increases as a function of time t according to the above equation.

content: The temperature of a blackbody radiation enclosed in a container of volume V is increased from 100$$^\circ$$C to 1000$$^\circ$$C. The heat required in the process is
explanation: Information in the statement of the questions is insufficient to calculate heat required to raise the temperature from 100$$^\circ$$C to 1000$$^\circ$$C.

content: Let $\bar{V}, V_{m s}, V_p$ denotes the mean speed, root mean square speed and most probable speed of the molecules each of mass $m$ in an ideal monoatomic gas at absolute temperature $T$ Kelvin. Which statement(s) is/are correct?

options.content: No molecules can have speed greater than $\sqrt{2} V_{m s}$.
No molecules can have speed less than $V_p / \sqrt{2}$.
Average kinetic energy of a molecule is $\frac{3}{4} m V_P^2$.

explanation:
The only correct statements are C and D. Here’s why:

Maxwell–Boltzmann speeds run from 0 to ∞, so there’s no hard cutoff at either end.

A is false (speeds can exceed any finite multiple of $V_{rms}$).

B is false (molecules can move arbitrarily slowly, down to $v\to0$).

The three characteristic speeds are

$$ V_p=\sqrt{\frac{2kT}{m}},\quad \overline V=\sqrt{\frac{8kT}{\pi m}},\quad V_{rms}=\sqrt{\frac{3kT}{m}}. $$

Numerically,

$$ V_p\approx1.414\,\sqrt{\tfrac{kT}{m}},\quad \overline V\approx1.596\,\sqrt{\tfrac{kT}{m}},\quad V_{rms}\approx1.732\,\sqrt{\tfrac{kT}{m}}, $$

so indeed

$$ V_p \;<\;\overline V\;<\;V_{rms}, $$

making C true.

Average kinetic energy per molecule is

$$ \tfrac12\,m\,V_{rms}^2 \;=\;\tfrac12\,m\!\bigl(\tfrac{3kT}{m}\bigr) \;=\;\tfrac32\,kT \;=\;\tfrac34\,m\,V_p^2, $$

so D is also true.

content: A cyclic process is shown in p-v diagram and T-S diagram. Which of the following statements is/are true?

options.content: Work done by the system in the complete cyclic process is non-zero.
The heat absorbed by the system in the complete cyclic process is non-zero.

explanation:
From $$p$$-$$V$$ diagram,

Process 1-2 is isobaric process.

Process 3-1 is isochoric process

From $$T$$-$$S$$ diagram,

Process 2-3 is constant entropy process

i.e., $$d S=0$$

$$\therefore \quad Q=T d S=0$$

$$\therefore$$ Process 2-3 is adiabatic process.

Also, in cyclic process

$$W_{\text {net }}=Q_{\text {net }} \neq 0$$

content: Consider the p - V diagram for 1 mole of an ideal monatomic gas shown in the figure. Which of the following statements is/are true?

options.content: The change in internal energy for the whole process is zero.
Heat is rejected during the process.
Change in internal energy for process A $$\to$$ B is $$ - {3 \over 2}$$p<sub>0</sub>V<sub>0</sub>.
Work done by the gas during the entire process is 2p<sub>0</sub>V<sub>0</sub>.

explanation:
Since, given p - V diagram is not a cyclic process, hence the change in internal energy for the whole process is not zero.


Heat rejected by the gas in path AB during isobaric compression,

$$d{Q_{AB}} = n{C_p}\Delta T$$

$$ = 1.{5 \over 2}R({T_B} - {T_A})$$ [$$\because$$ n = 1]

$$ = {{5R} \over 2}\left( {{{{p_0}{V_0}} \over {1 \times R}} - {{2{p_0}{V_0}} \over {1 \times R}}} \right) = - 5{{{p_0}{V_0}} \over 2}$$

Heat absorbed by the gas in path BC of isobaric process,

$$d{Q_{BC}} = n{C_v}\Delta T$$

$$ = 1 \times {{3R} \over 2}({T_C} - {T_B})$$

$$ = {{3R} \over 2}\left( {{{2{p_0}{V_0}} \over {1 \times R}} - {{{p_0}{V_0}} \over {1 \times R}}} \right) = {{3{p_0}{V_0}} \over 2}$$

Net heat $$ = d{Q_{AB}} + d{Q_{BC}}$$

$$ = {{ - 5{p_0}{V_0}} \over 2} + {{3{p_0}{V_0}} \over 2} = - {p_0}{V_0}$$

$$\therefore$$ Heat is rejected during the process.

Change in internal energy in process A $$\to$$ B,

$$\Delta U = n{C_v}\Delta T = 1 \times {3 \over 2}R({T_B} - {T_A})$$

$$ = {{3R} \over 2}\left( {{{{p_0}{V_0}} \over {1 \times R}} - {{2{p_0}{V_0}} \over {1 \times R}}} \right)$$

$$ = - {3 \over 2}{p_0}{V_0}$$

Work done by the gas during entire process is W, then

$$W = {W_{AB}} + {W_{BC}}$$

$$ = {p_0}({V_0} - 2{V_0}) + 0 = - {p_0}{V_0}$$

content: The initial pressure and volume of a given mass of an ideal gas with $$\left( {{{{C_p}} \over {{C_V}}} = \gamma } \right)$$, taken in a cylinder fitted with a piston, are p<sub>0</sub> and V<sub>0</sub> respectively. At this stage the gas has the same temperature as that of the surrounding medium which is T<sub>0</sub>. It is adiabatically compressed to a volume equal to $${{{V_0}} \over 2}$$. Subsequently the gas is allowed to come to thermal equilibrium with the surroundings. What is the heat released to the surrounding?
explanation:
The equation of state for an ideal gas undergoing adiabatic process is given as,

TV$$\gamma $$$$-$$1 = constant

Let the temperature after adiabatic compression as given in the question be T then,

$${T_0}{V^{\gamma - 1}} = T{\left( {{{{V_0}} \over 2}} \right)^{\gamma - 1}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow T = {T_0}{2^{\gamma - 1}}$$

Now, heat released at volume $${{{V_0}} \over 2}$$ to achieve temperature $${T_0}$$.

The net heat released can be determined by the equation,

$$\Delta Q = \mu {C_V}\Delta T$$

$$ = \mu \times {R \over {\gamma - 1}}$$ $$({T_0}{2^{\gamma - 1}} - {T_0})$$

$$ = {{\mu R{T_0}} \over {\gamma - 1}}({2^{\gamma - 1}} = 1)$$ $$ \because $$ $$(\mu {T_0}R = {p_0}{V_0})$$

$$ \therefore $$ Heat released = $${{{p_0}V} \over {\gamma - 1}}({2^{\gamma - 1}} - 1)$$

content:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are true?

"Internal energy of an ideal gas .............."

options.content: remains constant in an isothermal process.

explanation:
$$ \because $$ Internal energy of an ideal gas depends only on the temperature of gas.

Ein $$ \propto $$ T

content: If the pressure, temperature and density of an ideal gas are denoted by p, T and $$\rho $$, respectively, the velocity of sound in the gas is

options.content: proportional to $$\sqrt \rho  $$, when T is constant
proportional to $$\sqrt p $$, when $$\rho $$ is constant

explanation:
$$v = \sqrt {{{\gamma RT} \over M}} = \sqrt {{{\gamma P} \over \rho }} $$

(i) Velocity of sound is proportional to $$\sqrt T $$

$$ \therefore $$ $$v \propto \sqrt T $$

(ii) When $$\rho $$ is constant, the velocity of sound is proportional to root of pressure P

$$ \therefore $$ $$v \propto \sqrt P $$
